name: Wright-Patterson AFB
description: Wright-Patterson Air Force Base (WPAFB) in Ohio is a real U.S. Air Force installation with a long and documented history. However, it is also a central location in many UFO conspiracy theories. These theories primarily claim that WPAFB houses recovered alien spacecraft and extraterrestrial bodies, and that the base is involved in reverse-engineering alien technology. The alleged 'Hangar 18' is often mentioned, though its existence and purpose are disputed. Proponents point to unexplained sightings around the base, alleged witness testimonies from former military personnel claiming to have seen alien artifacts, and the Air Force's historical involvement with Project Blue Book (a public UFO investigation that concluded in 1969) as evidence. Mainstream perspective dismisses these claims as lacking verifiable evidence. The Air Force denies these claims, asserting that WPAFB primarily serves as a research and development, acquisition, and logistics center for the Air Force. Skeptics argue that supposed witness testimonies are often inconsistent or lack corroboration, and that the 'Hangar 18' story originated in a fictional movie. The base's legitimate secretive activities related to advanced military technology development contribute to the rumors, as does the historical association with Project Blue Book, which, some believe, was a cover-up operation.
subtitle: Site of alleged alien technology storage.

claims.claim: WPAFB houses recovered alien spacecraft and bodies.
claims.evidence: Alleged witness testimonies, unexplained sightings, rumors of 'Hangar 18'.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence, inconsistencies in testimonies, 'Hangar 18' origin in fiction.

claims.claim: The base is involved in reverse-engineering alien technology.
claims.evidence: Secrecy surrounding base activities, advanced technology research conducted there.
claims.criticism: Normal military research interpreted as alien reverse-engineering, absence of concrete proof.

claims.claim: Project Blue Book was a cover-up to hide alien encounters.
claims.evidence: Abrupt termination of Project Blue Book, perceived inconsistencies in official reports.
claims.criticism: Project Blue Book deemed inconclusive by the Air Force, alternative explanations for sightings exist.

keyFigures.name: Jesse Marcel
keyFigures.role: UFO researcher who handled debris from the Roswell incident and believed it to be extraterrestrial.

keyFigures.name: Stanton Friedman
keyFigures.role: Physicist and prominent UFO researcher who promoted the Roswell and alien technology theories.

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: Journalist and prominent UFO skeptic, known for debunking UFO claims.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to ancient astronaut theories, which suggest extraterrestrial influence on ancient civilizations.
Parallels with mythological accounts of encounters with beings from other realms.

timeline.event: Roswell Incident, leading to speculation about recovered alien technology.
Project Blue Book, the U.S. Air Force's official investigation of UFOs.
Publication of books and articles promoting the idea of WPAFB as a repository for alien artifacts.

sources.author: Berlitz, Charles, and William L. Moore
sources.title: Berlitz, Charles, and William L. Moore

sources.author: Friedman, Stanton T.
sources.work: Crash at Corona: The Definitive Study of the Roswell Incident
sources.title: Friedman, Stanton T.
